title: Who Lends Before Banking Crises? Evidence from the International Syndicated Loan Market
abstract: Existing studies assume that all lenders have similar incentives to take on risks during different phases of the lending cycle. We show that foreign lenders and low market share lenders extend more credit in comparison with other lenders during lending booms leading to banking crises but not during other credit expansions. These less established lenders also shorten loan maturity and increase the amount of credit they extend to riskier borrowers without asking for collateral or imposing covenants and higher interest rates. Our results suggest that foreign lenders and low market share lenders contribute disproportionately to credit misallocation and risk accumulation in precrisis periods.

author.Mariassunta Giannetti: Stockholm School of Economics, 113 83 Stockholm, Sweden; and Swedish House of Finance, 113 50 Stockholm, Sweden; and Center for Economic and Policy Research, Washington, District of Columbia 20009; and European Corporate Governance Institute, 1000 Brussels, Belgium
author.YeeJin Jang: University of New South Wales, Sydney, New South Wales 2052, Australia

article.1. Introduction: Credit expansions often lead to banking crises with significant negative consequences for the real economy (Reinhart and Rogoff 2009,Schularick and Taylor 2012). The common explanation is that excessive lending is associated with an increase in leverage and the funding of poor-quality borrowers (Gourinchas and Obstfeld 2012). However, whereas crises tend to be preceded by credit growth, credit growth may also be driven by an increase in investment opportunities or by an improvement in the financial sector’s ability to intermediate funds toward productive investment (Levine 2005). Therefore, finer predictions for discerning good and bad credit booms are crucial for macroprudential policies, which face a trade-off between financial stability and financial deepening (Ayyagari et al. 2017,Richter et al. 2019). To this end, several recent papers emphasize the importance of the allocation of credit as captured by narrower credit spreads (López-Salido et al. 2017), asset price bubbles (Greenwood et al. 2022), and loans to nontradable industries and households (Mian et al. 2017,Müller and Verner 2024). Lenders’ incentives and heterogeneity between lenders in the propensity to take risk have been largely neglected but could also play an important role in understanding why some credit booms end badly, whereas others do not. However, reliance on aggregate private credit and long-run data has so far prevented this investigation. This paper aims to shed light on the characteristics and incentives of the lenders that provide more credit in the years preceding banking crises and that appear to contribute to a larger extent to the accumulation of risk that limits an economy’s resilience. Considering lender heterogeneity is important to understand the mechanisms leading to banking crises. Some consider connected lending by well-established banks as the main factor behind the accumulation of bad loans and, consequently, of banking crises (e.g.,Krugman 1999,Brunnermeier and Reis 2019). On the other hand, new lenders may face more information asymmetry during credit booms and, being less experienced, become victims of optimistic expectations, which are deluded when the boom ends up in a bust (Gennaioli et al. 2015,Thakor 2015). In addition, atomistic agents may not internalize the externalities of high leverage on collateral prices and defaults (e.g.,Lorenzoni 2008,Farhi and Werning 2016,Korinek and Simsek 2016). Even though they do not consider lender heterogeneity, these theories provide another rationale for why less established lenders may be more prone to lend during credit booms that end up in busts. Less established lenders have limited prior exposure to a country and, consequently, place a small weight on the spillover effects of their decisions. In contrast, high market share lenders may internalize the negative spillovers of high leverage on the economy because of their current exposure and the impact on their future business. Based on the insights of existing theories, we test to what extent less established lenders, having less realistic expectations and not internalizing the negative spillovers of excessive risk taking, contribute to lending booms that precede banking crises. We consider foreign banks and all lenders with small market shares in a country to be less established. Using the chronology of banking crises ofBaron et al. (2021), we exploit lenders’ differential behavior in the international syndicated loan market. As we show, a country’s syndicated loan market experiences booms and busts around banking crises and, therefore, provides a suitable laboratory to explore differences in lender behavior during credit booms that end up in crises and other credit expansions in an international sample, which is essential to identify recurring patterns in banking crises. In addition, by focusing on syndicated loans, we consider relatively large borrowers from which negative spillovers on the rest of the economy are likely to originate. We show that, during the years preceding a banking crisis, well-established banks, which in the past have extended a large proportion of the loans in the country, provide less credit than other lenders. An increasing proportion of credit appears to be provided by foreign banks and low market share lenders.1We find no evidence that established lenders renew loans to existing inefficient borrowers, suggesting that connected lending is not the main driver of credit booms that precede banking crises. Instead, we observe that foreign lenders and low market share banks loosen their credit standards and increase the amount of loans they grant to borrowers that appear riskier and less profitable without asking for higher interest rates, collateral, or other covenants. Less established lenders also extend loans with short maturity, contributing to flighty debt and financial instability. Importantly, their behavior appears more pronounced during crises that are preceded by financial liberalizations and large real estate price appreciations. The association between lender behavior and banking crises accompanied by real estate price booms suggests that less established lenders favor credit misallocation also because less established lenders extend relatively more credit to borrowers in nontradable industries and in the real estate sectors. We find no differences in lending behavior between high market share banks and other lenders during credit booms that do not end up in banking crises or for crises that are more likely to have been driven by external factors, such as a drop in global risk appetite (proxied by an increase in the Volatility Index (VIX)). Finally, we show that the different behavior of more and less established lenders is more pronounced in industries to which these lenders extended lots of loans worldwide in the past. Put differently, during precrisis periods, established lenders extend relatively more credit to borrowers in industries to which they are less exposed. Risk accumulation in industries to which they lent less in the past can have smaller negative feedback effects on the lenders’ balance sheets because distress is known to cause severe negative externalities for other borrowers within a country’s industry (Carvalho 2015). In addition, all lenders are likely to know the industries to which they have lent a lot in other countries (Paravisini et al. 2023), especially because we focus on large borrowers in the syndicated loan market and exclude nontradable industries that tend to be country-specific. Thus, these results suggest that our findings are not merely driven by differences in expectations. Taken together, our results suggest that, at least to some extent, established lenders extend fewer loans in the years leading to banking crises because they internalize the negative consequences of risk accumulation in a country’s industry on their balance sheets. All our findings are obtained including interactions of bank and time fixed effects and of country and time fixed effects. Thus, the effects are identified considering banks’ differential lending behavior across different markets. Not only do the interactions of country and time fixed effects absorb the demand for credit within a country, but we also hold constant banks’ innate characteristics, such as their propensity to take risk and the regulation or monetary policy stance they face in their home country, thanks to the interactions of bank and time fixed effects. An increasing proportion of credit extended by less established lenders can be a useful indicator for the risk being accumulated in an economy regardless of whether it is demand- or supply-driven. However, we also show that less established lenders do not simply satisfy the demand of riskier borrowers that other lenders are unwilling to serve by focusing on within-loan credit supply: less established lenders tend to commit more capital during precrisis periods even when we absorb borrower heterogeneity by including loan fixed effects. Thus, our results are not merely driven by the matching of less established lenders with riskier borrowers, whose demand emerges during precrisis periods, but indicate that less established lenders supply more credit during precrisis periods. Our findings are important for macroprudential policy: by supplying more credit to riskier borrowers without offering more restrictive contracts and requiring higher interest rates, less established lenders contribute disproportionately to frothy market conditions. Thus, macroprudential policies should consider the type of lenders, not merely the quantity of credit. Our results provide a new rationale for why credit market concentration can enhance financial stability. The original argument is that competition distorts lenders’ risk-taking decisions by lowering their profit margins (Keeley 1990). We suggest that an increase in lender dispersion may indicate greater risk taking and a higher probability of banking crises. We also find that higher bank competition as captured by a lower concentration in the provision of credit is associated with lower lending standards on average as, for instance, inRuckes (2004). Differently from these theories, however, we highlight the importance of lender heterogeneity. In this respect, our work is related toFavara and Giannetti (2017)andGiannetti and Saidi (2019), who show that high market share banks internalize the negative spillovers associated with defaults and fire sales. We explore how lenders’ market shares affect their behavior during credit booms. Our paper also contributes to the literature on international capital flows. Important contributions in this literature highlight the destabilizing consequences of surges in bank debt led (gross) capital inflows, which are shown to be largely driven by global risk, global growth, global interest rates (Calvo et al. 1996;Forbes and Warnock 2012,2021), and global liquidity (Bruno and Shin 2013). We focus on foreign lenders, whose role is emphasized in international macroeconomic models but remains untested (Erten et al. 2021), and document that they are crucial in expanding credit and shortening maturity in crises-prone countries. At the same time, we abstract from the expansion of banks’ balance sheets (absorbed by the bank–time fixed effects) and the surge in capital inflows in the host country (absorbed by interactions of host country and time fixed effects), which have been emphasized in macrostudies of global capital flows. A related strand of the literature on international bank flows shows that the geography of bank lending changes in boom and crisis times (Cetorelli and Goldberg 2012a,b).2Whereas these studies emphasize financing and competitive conditions in the lenders’ markets, we focus on the host country and consider how the characteristics of the lenders vary in periods preceding banking crises. Finally, our paper is related to work showing how changes in debt composition and pricing predict growth, changes in aggregate credit, and bond returns. For instance,Mian et al. (2017)show that an increase in household debt to gross domestic product (GDP) ratio predicts lower GDP growth but that such a relation does not exist for corporate debt. On a different sample,Greenwood et al. (2022)stress that both household and corporate debt are important. Credit to nontradable sectors is also associated with booms and busts in output (Müller and Verner 2024).Krishnamurthy and Muir (2017)show that a decrease in credit spreads predicts financial crises.Greenwood and Hanson (2013)find that a deterioration in the average quality of firms with high debt issuance predicts poor performance of corporate bonds relative to Treasury bonds of similar maturity. We focus on the type of lenders rather than on the composition and quality of borrowers, which may be harder to ascertain ex ante.
article.2. Data Sources and Variable Definitions: 2.1. Dating Banking Crises We identify banking crises using the chronology ofBaron et al. (2021), who consider episodes of bank equity return declines in a country in excess of 30% during a year. Their approach allows us to consider episodes with salient crisis symptoms, such as panics and government interventions, and quieter periods of banking sector distress. As a result, the number of crises is larger than the number of crises identified by previous narrative accounts. Our sample includes 64 crises in 46 countries during 1986–2016. The various episodes are listed in Internet Table IA.I. Baron et al. (2021)show that the episodes of crises they identify are followed by credit contractions and output drops but are silent on the origins of these crises. Crises are largely a moment of truth that, because of some external shock, reveals how much risk has been accumulated in an economy. Our objective is to document which lenders contribute most to the build-up of debt and risk that limit an economy’s resilience and amplify the negative effects of the bust. Whether crises originate from bad lending policies or from a shock to fundamentals that reverses itself is not central to our findings even though we provide evidence that less established lenders may play a less important role when crises are driven by external factors. Building on the findings ofJordà et al. (2011), who show that five lags of annual credit growth have explanatory power for crises, we explore lending in the four years preceding the banking crisis year even though the patterns we highlight could already emerge a few years earlier. If another banking crisis occurs within this interval, we consider the years between two crises. 2.2. Bank Lending To observe how individual banks extend credit in a variety of countries, in the absence of data on other credit transactions with broad coverage, we resort to Dealscan, which covers international syndicated loan issuance. Syndicated loans represent a significant part of international bank claims (Gadanecz and Von Kleist 2002). Whereas syndicated lending is only a fraction of banks’ total lending, it is commonly used to evaluate bank lending policies and their real effects (e.g.,Acharya et al. 2018,2019;Bräuning and Ivashina 2020). More importantly, as shown in Internet Figure IA.1, there are booms and busts in the syndicated loan market, which coincide with the banking crises in our sample. This implies that the syndicated loan market is a relevant laboratory to explore changes in lender composition and behavior in a wide cross-section of countries. We extract data on all completed loans granted to publicly listed or privately held firms from 1986 to 2016. The sample period of each country starts in the year when the first loan is reported in Dealscan and ends in 2016.3All loan amounts are reported in U.S. dollars at the time of origination. We consider the bank holding company as the ultimate provider of credit. Our final sample includes 3,667 lenders, which offer loans to borrowers in 46 countries. Whereas 46% of the lenders are nonbank financial intermediaries, more than 90% of the loans are arranged by banks. Hence, our results must be interpreted as driven by banks. Because our main objective is to explore differences in lenders’ propensities to provide credit to borrowers in a country in periods preceding banking crises, we start by aggregating new loans issued at the lender–country–time level. The variableLoanb,c,tcaptures the amount of loans that lenderboriginates to borrowers headquartered in countrycduring yeart. A syndicated loan is extended jointly by a group of banks, including one or sometimes a couple of lead banks and several participant banks. Prior to signing the loan contract, lead banks assess the quality of the borrowers and negotiate terms and conditions. Once the main terms are in place, lead banks invite participant banks to acquire a stake of the loan but remain responsible for monitoring the borrower. We, thus, consider two alternative measures of loan provision. First, following existing literature (e.g.,Bharath et al. 2007,Ivashina and Scharfstein 2010), we consider lead arrangers to be the “lenders.” Such an assumption reflects the fact that lead arrangers are responsible for traditional bank duties, including due diligence, payment management, and monitoring of the loan. Second, we construct proxies for credit origination in which we consider all banks that committed capital using the loan shares in Dealscan. Because this information is not reported for two thirds of the sample, we follow a practice common in the literature of imputing loan shares when they are missing (see, e.g.,Chodorow-Reich 2014). If loan shares are missing, we set the loan shares equal to the average lead share in countrycfor lead lenders and divide the remaining amount of the loan equally among the other syndicate participants. In these instances, for multiple lead arrangers, we attribute an equal fraction of the lead arrangers’ total loan share to each lead arranger. Internet Table IA.II provides summary statistics for the main variables. Detailed variable definitions are in Internet Appendix A. 2.3. Lender Types Domestic, entrenched lenders are often thought to evergreen loans to inefficient borrowers and to their cronies, accumulating losses that subsequently lead to banking crises. If this were the case, we would expect foreign lenders and, in general, less established lenders in a country to lend less during lending booms that end up in banking crises. However, episodes of excessive lending may also be driven by lenders that have less precise information or do not internalize the consequences of their actions on other borrowers and the overall economy. In this respect, foreign lenders are more likely to be less informed. As is common in the literature (see, e.g.,Giannetti and Laeven 2012a), we define foreign lenders as banks whose headquarters are based in a foreign country. We also consider that lenders with low market shares in a country are likely to have limited local knowledge and less business at stake in the host country. As a result, they may not fully internalize the spillover effects of their actions and may take more risks. On the other hand, these lenders, not burdened by past relationships, may contribute less to the accumulation of bad loans. FollowingGiannetti and Saidi (2019), we construct two proxies for a lender’s market share in a country. Our first proxy captures that excessive risk taking may generate defaults, whose effects are amplified and affect other borrowers’ ability to repay. Only lenders with a large fraction of the loans outstanding in a country on their balance sheets internalize the negative spillovers of defaults. We, thus, capture this effect using the share of outstanding loans retained by lenderbin countrycin yeart,Retained Shareb,c,t, defined as the dollar amount of loans committed and retained by a lender that have not yet reached maturity, divided by the dollar amount of all loans issued to borrowers in a country that have not yet reached maturity. Because the allocation of the loan between participants in the syndicate is not always reported, we resort to imputing loan shares as we do for the loan provision proxies based on committed credit. Our second proxy for a lender’s market share isArranged Shareb,c,t, defined as the volume of loans arranged by lenderbover the total volume of loans issued in countrycin yeart. Besides a lender’s better information, this proxy captures the lender’s incentives arising from the current and future profits it expects to generate in a country. We attribute the total loan amount to the lead arranger unless there are multiple lead arrangers in which case we divide the loan amount equally among the lead arrangers. In Internet Table IA.II, according to both measures, the average market share, equal to 0.002, is small and reflects that many lenders only sporadically issue loans in a country. This low average also reflects our choice to consider any lenders that extend at least one loan in a country during the sample period. The proxies for average market shares are, however, highly skewed. Lenders in the top percentile have arranged more than 6% of the loans in a country. Their share of the syndicated loan market, which presumably also reflects other activities that a bank carries out in the host country, is such that these lenders may have incentives to internalize any spillovers of excess leverage. At the same time, in the aggregate, the share of low market share lenders and foreign lenders in a country is large enough that these lenders could credibly contribute to risk accumulation and misallocation and have macroeconomic effects. For instance, in the aggregate, foreign lenders commit 78% of the total syndicated loan credit in a country. Similarly, banks with market share less than 1% extend 32% of the syndicated loan credit.Figure 1vividly illustrates that the proportion of credit extended by foreign and low market share banks is larger during precrisis periods than in normal times even though differences are not statistically significant given the large heterogeneity in the syndicated loan market across countries.4The figure also confirms that foreign and low market share lenders represent a significant portion of the syndicated loan market arriving to extend up to 70% of the loans during precrisis periods. In what follows, we exploit within-country differences in lenders’ behavior to evaluate whether less established lenders extend more loans during precrisis periods controlling for the country’s demand for credit. 2.4. Borrowers’ and Loan Characteristics Besides considering the quantity of credit provided by different lenders to borrowers in a country, we infer a lender’s propensity to take risk considering borrower and loan characteristics. In particular, we consider the proportion of loans to borrowers of a specific type issued by a given lender during a year out of all loans granted to borrowers of that type in a country during that year. To identify borrower characteristics, we merge the loan-level information from Dealscan with Global Compustat using the borrowers’ names and locations. We are able to obtain financial information for publicly listed borrowers as of the most recent fiscal year prior to the loan issuance date. We consider loans to unprofitable and inefficient borrowers, defined as borrowers with return on assets (ROA) and sales per employee in the bottom quartile of a given country and year. Moreover, we consider as particularly risky loans to small borrowers, defined as borrowers with assets in the bottom quartile of our sample in a given country and year; loans to borrowers with high leverage, defined as borrowers with leverage in the top quartile of our sample in a given country and year; and loans to borrowers with low interest rate coverage, defined as loans to borrowers with interest rate coverage in the bottom quartile of the sample in a given country and year. We also examine loans to private firms and loans to firms that are unrated, which tend to be riskier. To have an alternative measure of a borrower’s risk, we manually match estimates of corporate default probabilities from the National University of Singapore–Risk Management Institute Credit Research Initiative with borrowers in the syndicated loan market. Finally, we consider how lenders’ characteristics are associated with loan terms and how this relationship varies during precrisis periods. In particular, we consider loans without covenants, unsecured loans, and loans with short maturity, defined as loans with maturity in the bottom quartile for that country and year. To examine whether lenders impose different interest rates during precrisis periods, we obtain the all-in-drawn spread over the London Interbank Offered Rate (LIBOR) from Dealscan and consider the average interest rates of loans extended by a lender to different borrowers, weighted using the loan amount.
article.3. Methodology: Our objective is to estimate how the composition of lenders in a given country varies over time. In particular, we test whether lenders with certain characteristics extend more credit in periods leading to banking crises. We estimate the following equation: Therefore, our empirical tests capture how the composition of credit in a country varies in different phases of the lending cycle, holding constant bank-level supply shocks and host country–level shocks. In practice,𝛽1β1compares the differential behavior of foreign lenders and high market share lenders with the behavior of these lenders in normal years because crisis years are too infrequent to affect the average effect of𝐿𝑒𝑛𝑑𝑒𝑟_𝑐ℎ𝑎𝑟𝑏,𝑐,𝑡−1.Lender_charb,c,t−1.
article.4. Results: 4.1. Main Findings Table 1explores how foreign lenders and lenders with different market shares extend loans to countries during precrisis years. Panel A shows a clear increase in foreign lenders’ loan issuance during precrisis years. The effect is not only statistically, but also economically significant. In column (1), the amount of loans arranged by foreign lenders during precrisis periods increases by about 50% relative to the average amount of loans arranged by a bank in a country (1.96) in our sample; also, in column (2), the probability that a foreign lender arranges any loans in a country during the precrisis years increases by nearly 50% with respect to the unconditional probability of any lender arranging a loan in a country (0.1). The coefficient estimates are even larger in columns (3) and (4), in which we consider the amount of credit committed by each lender. Table 1.Lending During Precrisis Periods Table 1.Lending During Precrisis Periods Notes.This table explores loan issuance by different types of lenders during precrisis periods. The estimates are from ordinary least squares (OLS) regressions using the lender–country–year panel. Panel A shows lending during the precrisis periods by foreign lenders, and panel B shows the propensity to extend credit of lenders with different market shares. The dependent variables are different proxies for loan provision by lenderbto countrycin yeart. In panel B,Market Shareis based onRetained Sharein columns (1)–(4) and onArranged Sharein columns (5)–(8).Market Shareis lagged by one year. All regressions include interactions of lender and year fixed effects and of country and year fixed effects. The definitions of all variables are provided in Internet Appendix A. Standard errors are clustered by lender. Robustt-statistics are shown in parentheses. ***, **, and * denote significance at the 1%, 5%, and 10% levels, respectively. Importantly, not only is the effect economically significant from an individual bank’s point of view, but it is amplified in the aggregate by the fact that the syndicated loan market is dominated by foreign lenders. The median (average) country in our sample has 316 (355) foreign banks that have committed credit at some point during the sample period; in the median (average) country, 53 (63) foreign banks extend credit during a year. Thus, the estimates in column (3) indicate that, whereas the average foreign bank extends 32% more credit in a precrisis country (equivalent to a 46 million increase relative to the 144 million average amount of credit committed by a bank in a country), in the aggregate, this translates in an increase in credit of 16.3 billion (16,330 = 46 × 55). This indicates that, in the aggregate, during precrisis periods, foreign banks can greatly contribute to the credit expansion. In panel B, we consider the past market share of a lender in a country instead of the nationality of the lender’s headquarters. Even though high market share lenders extend more credit on average, loan extension during precrisis periods is concentrated among less established lenders. The result is robust when we use different proxies for loan provision and market share. It is also both economically and statistically significant. For instance, in column (5), decreasing a lender’s arranged share by two standard deviations (equal to 0.04) is associated with an increase in lending during precrisis periods in comparison with normal times by nearly 25% relative to the average amount of loans arranged by a bank in a country (1.96). Importantly, countries have plenty of low market share banks: for instance, in the average country, we have 396 banks with market share below 1%, and 60 of those extend credit during the typical year, indicating that their propensity to extend credit can greatly contribute to lending booms preceding banking crises.5 Overall, given that the demand for credit within a country and a bank’s ability to extend new loans are held constant by the high-dimension fixed effects we include in the regressions, it appears that credit expansions during precrisis periods are largely driven by lenders that are not well-established in the host country. Our estimates are qualitatively and quantitatively similar when we consider different proxies for credit provision and market share. For brevity, henceforth, we present estimates only for the empirical models that rely on the amount of credit committed and theRetained Share. 4.2. Lending During Crises and Good Credit Booms InTable 1, we interpret differences in behavior between lenders to arise in precrisis periods in comparison with normal times, largely because most observations in non-precrisis periods consist of normal times rather than actual crisis years. Concerns may arise, however, that our results are driven by the fact that established lenders are more inclined to provide liquidity during bad times asGiannetti and Saidi (2019)highlight during episodes of industry distress. This could lead us to understate their contribution to precrisis lending booms.Table 2explores whether this is the case. Columns (1) and (2) test whether foreign lenders provide less credit during crisis years, whereas columns (3) and (4) focus on the lenders’ market shares. We find no evidence that established lenders provide more credit during crisis periods. Foreign banks do. Table 2.Crises and Precrisis Periods Table 2.Crises and Precrisis Periods Notes.This table considers differences in lending during banking crises. The estimates are from OLS regressions using the lender–country–year panel. Together with thePrecrisisindicator, we includeCrisis, the indicator for the banking crisis year as defined inBaron et al. (2021). Market share is theRetained Share, and loan provision is based on lenders’ propensity to commit credit. All regressions include interactions of lender and year fixed effects and of country and year fixed effects. The definitions of all variables are provided in Internet Appendix A. Standard errors are clustered by lender. Robustt-statistics are shown in parentheses. ***, **, and * denote significance at the 1%, 5%, and 10% levels, respectively. These results support our interpretation that differences in lending behavior arise during precrisis years relative to normal times. They also indicate that widespread distress and inability to tap external finance prevent high market share banks from providing liquidity during banking crises, suggesting that they may be unable to reduce fire sales as they do when distress affects a single industry (Giannetti and Saidi 2019). Second, one may wonder whether foreign and low market share lenders’ behavior is typical of periods preceding banking crises. In particular, newcomers could expand credit when there is high demand for credit in a country because high market share lenders have reached full capacity.Table 3evaluates this possibility considering credit booms that are not followed by banking crises. We consider a country to experience a credit boom if it experiences an annualized change in private credit to GDP over three years in the top 25% of the country over the sample period. The sample includes 214 years that are classified as credit booms in 40 countries. Thus, credit booms are slightly more frequent than precrisis periods. We exclude any precrisis years from the sample and repeat the tests in panels A and B ofTable 1with theCredit Boomvariable. We find no evidence that, during credit expansions that do not result in banking crises, foreign lenders and low market share lenders provide more credit. If anything, columns (1) and (2) show that foreign lenders provide less credit than during normal times. Table 3.Lending During Good Credit Booms Table 3.Lending During Good Credit Booms Notes.This table considers lending booms that do not result in banking crises. The estimates are from OLS regressions in the lender–country–year panel.Credit Boomis an indicator variable that equals one during years in which a country’s annualized change in private credit to GDP ratio over the previous three years is in the top 25% of the sample period within the country and zero otherwise. Precrisis year observations are excluded. Market share is theRetained Share, and loan provision is based on lenders’ propensity to commit credit. Because of data availability, the following borrowing countries are not included in the sample: Egypt, Iceland, Peru, Philippines, Taiwan, and Venezuela. All regressions include interactions of lender and year fixed effects and of country and year fixed effects. The definitions of all variables are provided in Internet Appendix A. Standard errors are clustered by lender. Robustt-statistics are shown in parentheses. ***, **, and * denote significance at the 1%, 5%, and 10% levels, respectively. 4.3. Old and New Customers and the Supply of Credit We also explore why less established lenders appear to extend more credit during precrisis periods. Less established lenders could satisfy the demand of new borrowers that established banks are unable to satisfy because of capacity constraints. Established banks may also be unwilling to establish relationships with new borrowers if these are perceived to be riskier or poor quality, whereas less established banks would do so to increase their market shares. Considering the extension of loans to old borrowers also allows us to evaluate the possibility that domestic lenders and high market share lenders contribute to the accumulation of bad loans even though they provide less credit than other lenders. Established lenders could extend and renew loans to inefficient relationship borrowers, ultimately driving down aggregate productivity and contributing to the financial crisis (Brunnermeier and Reis 2019). To begin assessing this possibility, we examine whether changes in lending behavior during the lending cycle are more pronounced when banks provide loans to borrowers with which they have previous relationships compared with nonrelationship borrowers. We define bank–borrower relationships considering whether a bank has arranged a loan for a particular borrower during the previous five years because the lead bank acquires information and interacts with the borrower, whereas other lenders in the syndicate act as transaction lenders. In panel A of Internet Table IA.V, we consider banks’ loans to relationship borrowers, defined as loans to borrowers to which a bank has arranged at least one loan during the previous five years. In panel B, we consider loans to borrowers that are most likely to be new bank clients, defined as loans to borrowers to which a bank had not arranged any loan during the previous five years.6It appears that foreign and low market share banks are more inclined to lend to both new and old borrowers. The coefficients in column (1) of panels A and B imply that foreign banks extend even more credit to new borrowers during precrisis periods (the difference in coefficients on the interaction term is statistically significant at the 1% level), whereas we find no difference in high market share banks’ propensity to extend credit to relationship and nonrelationship borrowers during precrisis periods. This evidence suggests that the lending booms preceding banking crises are unlikely to be driven by loan evergreening and connected lending as these imply disproportionate lending to old borrowers by established banks. However, the results could suggest that less established banks satisfy the demand for credit by the riskiest borrowers whether new or old. To isolate the supply of credit, we consider lenders’ propensities to commit capital within the same syndicate. Differential propensities to fund the same loan allow us to abstract from borrower heterogeneity and to test whether less established lenders commit more capital in precrisis periods independently from the risk of the loan, which we hold constant by including loan fixed effects.Table 4reports the results. The proportion of a loan that is provided by foreign banks increases in precrisis periods (columns (1) and (2)). Conversely, high market share banks commit less capital (columns (3) and (4)). The estimates are obtained by controlling for the status of a lender as a lead lender within the syndicate. This allows us to exclude that our results are driven by the propensity of lenders with different characteristics to act as lead arrangers during precrisis periods. The estimates demonstrate that less established lenders are willing to supply more credit to the same borrower for precisely the same loan, indicating that less established lenders do not merely satisfy the demand of riskier borrowers. Table 4.Within-Syndicate Credit Provision: Controlling for Borrower Heterogeneity Table 4.Within-Syndicate Credit Provision: Controlling for Borrower Heterogeneity Notes.This table explores different lenders’ propensity to commit capital within the same syndicate and controls for loan fixed effects. We construct a syndicate-lender panel that includes all syndicated loans issued during 1986–2016 with loan share information available. The estimates are from OLS regressions, for which the dependent variable isLoan Share, the proportion of the syndicated loan amount extended by each lender. Market share is theRetained Share. All regressions include loan fixed effects. Columns (2) and (4) also include interactions of lender country and year fixed effects. The definitions of all variables are provided in Internet Appendix A. Standard errors are clustered by loan package. Robustt-statistics are shown in parentheses. ***, **, and * denote significance at the 1%, 5%, and 10% levels, respectively. 4.4. Borrower Quality and Risk Taking Ultimately, credit expansions weaken financial stability if they lower credit standards. Our microdata allow us to document which lenders relax lending standards to a larger extent during precrisis periods. If foreign lenders and low market share lenders indeed contribute to increase financial fragility in the host country, we should observe that they lend to low-quality borrowers and take more risk during precrisis periods. Moreover, if established lenders are contributing to risk accumulation and credit misallocation, we should observe that they also lend to unprofitable borrowers. We consider the characteristics of the borrowers receiving loans from different types of lenders. We also explore whether lenders attempt to mitigate credit risk by asking for collateral and imposing covenants on the borrowers. We ask which lenders issue more loans to low-quality or risky borrowers in a country during a year. The dependent variable is the proportion of low-quality or risky loans committed by a given lender out of all low-quality or risky loans issued in a given country during that period. We provide various definitions of low-quality and risky loans. We start considering that loans to firms in nontradable industries tend to be riskier because these firms cannot export when internal demand collapses (see, e.g.,Desai et al. 2008) and accordingly have been documented to experience large-scale defaults during financial crises (Müller and Verner 2024). We identify nontradable industries using the classification ofMian and Sufi (2014). We also differentiate credit provided to construction and real estate companies from credit to other nontradable industries because companies in construction and real estate are typically more susceptible to risk during crisis periods and may result in greater losses for the lender. In addition, we define risky loans based on a low distance to default, small borrower’s size, high leverage, low interest rate coverage, unrated borrowers, and unlisted borrowers. We further capture low-quality borrowers as borrowers with low profitability and low productivity. All empirical specifications include interactions of lender and time fixed effects as well as interactions of country and time fixed effects. Hence, the estimates are obtained holding constant a lender’s propensity to take on risk across countries at a given point in time and country-level shocks. Panel A ofTable 5shows that foreign lenders have more cautious lending policies on average. This indicates that banks do not generally take more risk abroad, where they might be lightly monitored by domestic regulators. Foreign banks increase the amount of loans that they commit to risky borrowers relative to other lenders only during precrisis periods.7Not only do foreign lenders increase the proportion of loans they extend to firms in nontradable industries in a country during precrisis periods, but they extend relatively riskier loans along all dimensions we consider. The effects are not only statistically, but also economically, significant. For example, the estimates in column (1) imply that the proportion of loans committed by a foreign lender to borrowers in nontradable industries increases by nearly 50% during precrisis periods relative to normal times. Interestingly, within nontradable industries, foreign lenders extend more loans to borrowers in real estate than in other less cyclical nontradable industries. The percentage of loans to real estate borrowers committed by foreign lenders during precrisis periods increases three times relative to normal times, whereas credit to other nontradable industries just doubles. In column (4), the percentage of loans to borrowers with low distance to default by foreign lenders more than doubles during precrisis periods relative to normal times. In columns (8) and (9), foreign lenders commit nearly 50% more loans to unrated and private borrowers during precrisis periods. Table 5.Banks’ Propensity to Lend to Borrowers with Different Characteristics Table 5.Banks’ Propensity to Lend to Borrowers with Different Characteristics Notes.This table shows how different types of lenders provide credit to borrowers with different characteristics during the precrisis period. The estimates are from OLS regressions in the lender–country–year panel. Panel A considers foreign lenders, and panel B considers lenders with different market shares. The dependent variables are different proxies for credit provision by lenderbto countrycin yeartto borrowers with different characteristics.% Commit [borrower_char]is defined as the percentage of credit committed to borrowers with specific characteristics by lenderbin countrycin yeartout of all loans with that characteristic in countrycand yeart. If lenderbdoes not commit loans in yeart, the %Commitvariables are set to zero.[borrower_char]includes borrowers inNontradableindustries, in which nontradable industries are further split intoReal Estate(SIC codes 1500–1700, 6500–6900) andNontradable Other than Real Estate(SIC codes 5200–5900), withLow Distance to Default(distance to default in the bottom 25%),Smallfirms (firm size in the bottom 25%), borrowers withHigh Leverage(leverage in the top 25%),Low Interest Coverage(earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA)/interest expense in the bottom 25%),UnratedandPrivateborrowers and borrowers withLow Profitability(ROA in the bottom 25%) andLow Productivity(sales scaled by the number of employees in the bottom 25%). The distribution of borrower characteristics is estimated for each countrycin yeartconsidering observations with available borrower information. In panel B,Market Shareis a bank’sRetained Shareand is lagged by one year. All regressions include interactions of lender and year fixed effects and of country and year fixed effects. The definitions of all variables are provided in Internet Appendix A. Standard errors are clustered by lender. Robustt-statistics are shown in parentheses. ***, **, and * denote significance at the 1%, 5%, and 10% levels, respectively. Not only do foreign lenders extend credit to riskier borrowers during precrisis periods, but they also lend less efficiently. In columns (10) and (11), it appears that, on average, foreign lenders extend fewer loans to low profitability and low productivity borrowers as is consistent with studies that highlight that foreign banks, unencumbered by political connections, allocate credit more efficiently (see, e.g.,Giannetti and Ongena 2009). However, their behavior changes during precrisis periods when they increase the proportion of loans they extend to low-profitability and low-productivity borrowers in comparison with normal times. Panel B repeats the empirical tests for lenders with different market shares. Unsurprisingly, banks with higher market shares provide a larger proportion of loans to riskier and inefficient borrowers on average. However, during precrisis periods, these lenders decrease the proportion of loans to riskier and inefficient borrowers they extend in comparison with normal times. In column (1), a one standard deviation increase in the retained share is associated with a 0.25 percentage point smaller proportion of loans committed to borrowers in nontradable industries during the precrisis period, which is 14% of the dependent variable’s standard deviation. During precrisis periods, low market share lenders extend more credit to borrowers in the real estate sector compared with other nontradable industries. In columns (4) and (6), a one standard deviation increase in a lender’s retained share is associated with a decrease in the lender’s proportion of loans committed during precrisis periods by 116% for low distance to default borrowers and by 80% for highly levered borrowers with respect to the dependent variable’s unconditional mean. High market share lenders also extend less credit to low-profitability and low-productivity borrowers, suggesting that entrenched credit extension by established banks is unlikely to drive the banking crisis. These tests indicate that foreign lenders and low market share lenders contribute to a larger extent than other lenders to the accumulation of risk, which ultimately results in financial instability. Together with the earlier finding that less established banks lend more to both new and old borrowers, these results indicate that connected lending by established banks contributes little to capital misallocation in precrisis periods. Because these are periods of high growth during which the corporate and banking sectors are doing well (Jordà et al. 2017), the lack of evidence of capital misallocation by established banks is consistent with the view that zombie lending is the consequence of large economic shocks, regulatory forbearance, and an undercapitalized banking sector (seeAcharya et al. 2022for a survey) and does not support the theoretical mechanisms put forward byKrugman (1999)andBrunnermeier and Reis (2019). 4.5. Loan Contractual Characteristics Foreign lenders and low market share lenders could attempt to reduce the risk arising from their loans to risky and low-quality borrowers by requiring collateral and imposing other covenants on the borrowers. Alternatively, they could extend loans with shorter maturity, which give the lenders more control rights but at the same time contribute to financial instability. Table 6shows that the proportion of loans extended by less established lenders that are unsecured and have no covenant increases during precrisis periods, providing further support to the notion that foreign lenders become less cautious during credit booms that end up in busts. We also consider the proportion of short-term loans committed by different lenders to borrowers in a country, defined as the proportion of loans with maturity in the bottom quartile for that country and year. Foreign and low market share lenders appear to disproportionately shorten the maturity of the loans they extend during precrisis periods. This finding indicates that less established lenders contribute disproportionately to the short-term flows, which generate large negative externalities because of the difficulty of rolling over debt during crises (Korinek 2018). Table 6.Contract Terms Table 6.Contract Terms Notes.This table shows how different types of lenders provide credit to borrowers in the precrisis period. The estimates are from OLS regressions in the lender–country–year panel. Panel A considers foreign lenders, and panel B considers lenders with different market shares.% Commit [loan_char]is defined as the percentage of credit committed to loans with specific contract terms by lenderbin countrycin yeartout of all loans with that characteristic in countrycand yeart. If lenderbdoes not commit loans in yeart, the %Commitvariables are set to zero.[loan_char]includes loans withNo Covenant,Unsecuredloans, and loans withShort Maturity. A loan is considered to have short maturity if its maturity is in bottom 25% for that country and year and is only defined for loans with available maturity information. In panel B,Market Shareis a bank’sRetained Shareand is lagged by one year. All regressions include interactions of lender and year fixed effects and of country and year fixed effects. The definitions of all variables are provided in Internet Appendix A. Standard errors are clustered by lender. Robustt-statistics are shown in parentheses. ***, **, and * denote significance at the 1%, 5%, and 10% levels, respectively. 4.6. Loan Interest Rate Foreign lenders and low market share lenders may correctly price the risk of bank loans even though they serve marginal borrowers. To evaluate this possibility,Table 7explores whether less established lenders charge a premium on the loans they extend during precrisis periods. In panel A, we find no evidence that this is the case for foreign lenders both when we explore the effect on the average interest rate in column (1) and when we look at the average interest rates on subsets of loans to riskier borrowers in the subsequent columns. We find no differences even when we consider the average interest rate that a lender charges on loans with short maturity, indicating that the lack of risk premium is not driven by the fact that foreign lenders extend short-term loans. If anything, foreign lenders provide loans at lower interest rates to risky and low-quality borrowers during precrisis periods as we observe that the average interest rate on loans they extend to real estate, low distance to default, high-leverage, low interest rate coverage, and low-profitability borrowers is lower, suggesting that foreign lenders are not being compensated for the risk they take. Table 7.Loan Interest Rates Table 7.Loan Interest Rates Notes.This table shows the average interest rates of loans by lender type. The estimates are from OLS regressions in the lender–country–year panel. Panel A considers foreign lenders, and panel B lenders with different market shares. The dependent variable,Average Spread, is the value-weighted all-in-drawn spread of loans committed by lenderbto countrycin yeart, weighted by the loan amount retained. In columns (2)–(8),Average Spreadis computed using the subsample of loans committed by lenderbto countrycin yeartto borrowers/loans with specific characteristics, which include borrowers inNontradableindustries, withLow Distance to Default(distance to default in the bottom 25%),Smallfirms (firm size in the bottom 25%), borrowers withHigh Leverage(leverage in the top 25%),Low Interest Coverage(EBITDA/interest expense in the bottom 25%),Unrated(firm without S&P bond rating) andPrivateborrowers, borrowers withLow Profitability(ROA in the bottom 25%), andLow Sales to Employee(sales scaled by the number of employees in the bottom 25%), and loans withNo Covenant,Unsecuredloans, and loans withShort Maturity. A loan is considered to have short maturity if its maturity is in bottom 25% for that country and year and is defined only for loans with available maturity information. The dependent variables are computed considering only observations with spread information available. In panel B,Market Shareis based onRetained Shareand is lagged by one year. All regressions include interactions of lender and year fixed effects and of country and year fixed effects. The definitions of all variables are provided in Internet Appendix A. Standard errors are clustered by lender. Robustt-statistics are shown in parentheses. ***, **, and * denote significance at the 1%, 5%, and 10% levels, respectively. In panel B, we perform similar exercises considering high market share lenders. We find that high market share lenders extend loans at similar rates as other banks even though they appear to take less risk. High market share banks appear to charge higher rates to borrowers with high leverage and real estate companies. Thus, they are not only less inclined to lend to riskier borrowers, but they also incorporate risk to a larger extent when they do so. These results resonate with the findings ofKrishnamurthy and Muir (2017)that credit spreads decrease in periods leading to financial instability. Our empirical findings show that a higher supply of credit from less established lenders appears to prevent risk from being correctly priced.
article.5. Macroeconomic Implications: 5.1. Crisis Characteristics We have so far documented that foreign and low market share banks tend to extend more loans and take more risk during precrisis periods. However, episodes of banking crises differ markedly (Reinhart and Rogoff 2009). Whereas, in a few cases, crises appear to be driven by external shocks and drops in the global risk appetite, which tend to have large negative effects in financially fragile economies, other times, the causes are internal. We test to what extent less established lenders’ behavior plays a more important role for crises that are more likely to have been driven by internal reasons. Lending booms are believed to go bust because capital is misallocated to nontradable sectors and real estate in particular (Müller and Verner 2024). In panel A ofTable 8, we identify crises that are preceded by a boom in the real estate prices if the average annualized growth in the real estate prices of the country during the precrisis period is in the top decile of the annual real estate price returns among all countries during our sample period.8In columns (1) and (2), it is apparent that foreign lenders extend significantly more credit before crises that have been preceded by a real estate boom. Not only is the coefficient onPrecrisis with Housing Boom×Foreign Lenderpositive and statistically significant, but it indicates that foreign lenders’ propensity to extend credit in the years before banking crises that are preceded by a housing boom is almost five times larger than during other crises. In columns (3) and (4), we consider high market share banks. We find that their lower propensity to lend during precrisis periods is largely driven by crises that are preceded by a real estate boom. Together with the finding inTable 6that less established banks extend disproportionately more credit to borrowers in nontradable sectors and real estate during precrisis periods, these results suggest that less established banks may favor the credit misallocation that ultimately results in a banking crisis. Table 8.Banking Crisis Characteristics Table 8.Banking Crisis Characteristics Notes.This table considers several characteristics of banking crises. In panel A, we consider banking crises that have been accompanied by a housing price boom.Precrisis with Housing Boomis a dummy variable that takes value one if the average annualized growth in the real estate prices of the country during the precrisis period is in the top decile of the annual real estate price returns of the 16 countries for which real estate prices are available over 1986–2016 from the Jordà–Schularick–Taylor Macrohistory Database. The sample is restricted to 16 countries with available real estate price data. In panel B, we consider banking crises that have been preceded by a financial liberalization.Precrisis with Liberalizationis an indicator variable that takes value one if either the bank entry requirement index or the official supervisory power index of a borrower country fromBarth et al. (2004)decreased over the 10 years prior to the crisis period. The sample is restricted to the period 1999–2015 because of the availability of the regulation indices. In panel C, we consider crises that have presumably been driven by external factors as captured by the VIX index.Precrisis High VIXis an indicator that takes value one if during the crisis period we observe a monthly change in the VIX index in the top 1% of the distribution of monthly observations from 1986 to 2016. The estimates are from OLS regressions in the lender–country–year panel. Market share is theRetained Shareand loan provision is based on lenders’ propensities to commit credit. All regressions include interactions of lender and year fixed effects and of country and year fixed effects. The definitions of all variables are provided in Internet Appendix A. Standard errors are clustered by lender. Robustt-statistics are shown in parentheses. ***, **, and * denote significance at the 1%, 5%, and 10% levels, respectively. Panel B considers that destabilizing credit booms that end up in banking crises often occur after episodes of financial liberalization (see, e.g.,Hellmann et al. 2000,Giannetti 2007), which are followed by lending booms and sudden stops upon the realization that banks had lent to low-quality borrowers. We define financial liberalizations if either the bank entry requirement index or the official supervisory power index of a borrower country fromBarth et al. (2004)decreased over the 10 years prior to the crisis. The estimates in panel B suggest that foreign lenders and low market share lenders indeed extend more credit before crises that follow financial liberalizations. Because financial liberalizations often end up in banking crises because of credit misallocation and accumulation of bad loans, these findings are consistent with the evidence in panel A. Finally, in panel C, we consider crises that are more likely to have been driven by a drop in global risk appetite, originating from the United States and largely outside credit markets, during the crisis year. We capture such a drop in global risk appetite with an increase in the VIX index in the top 1% of the distribution of monthly observations from 1986 to 2016. The estimates suggest that less established lenders lend less during credit booms that are more likely to have ended in banking crises because of a global drop in risk appetite. To the extent that these lending booms ended in a bust because of external circumstances, these results indicate that less established lenders contribute more to credit booms that end up in a bust because of credit misallocation. 5.2. Crisis Prediction Our focus so far has been to understand crises by studying the lending booms that precede them, asSufi and Taylor (2022)suggest, instead of estimating models of crisis prediction (e.g.,Schularick and Taylor 2012). This is more appropriate in our context because we observe only a segment of the credit market. Whereas it is common for researchers to generalize from the syndicated loan market to broader credit decisions, the power to predict macroeconomic events may be reduced. Moreover, whereas our data allow us to document how different lenders extend credit during precrisis periods, we have to rely on a relatively short sample, which starts in 1986.9The short sample does not allow us to perform an out-of-sample prediction of banking crises. Nevertheless, we attempt to quantify the extent to which taking into account the extension of credit by less established lenders can improve crisis prediction. We estimate the probability that a countrycexperiences a crisis at timet,considering the extent to which credit growth has been driven by a change in the composition of credit. Specifically, we construct a country–year panel and estimate the following equation: InTable 9, both proxies for the credit provision by less established lenders help predicting banking crises in accordance with our hypotheses. The effects are not only statistically, but also economically significant. For instance, in column (1), a one standard deviation increase in the change of credit to GDP (0.20) increases the probability of a banking crisis by 4.0 percentage points. In column (2), the same probability goes from 1.9 percentage points, when the increase in loans arranged by foreign banks is in the bottom quartile (−0.32), to 6.7 percentage points when the increase in the loans extended by foreign banks is in the top quartile (1.01). Importantly, theR2increases by nearly three percentage points when we add to column (1) the variables on the quality of lending, indicating that considering this feature of lending booms can improve early warning models of financial crises. The average market share of the banks extending credit in a country also contributes significantly to predict banking crises. In column (4), the probability of a banking crisis increases by two percentage points if a one standard deviation increase in the change of credit to GDP is accompanied by a decrease in the average market share of the banks extending credit from the top quartile of (0.023) to the bottom quartile (0.012). This effect is equivalent to a 42% increase relative to the mean probability of a banking crisis. Table 9.Predicting Banking Crises Table 9.Predicting Banking Crises Notes.We estimate linear regression with probability models to predict banking crises using a country–year panel during the period 1986–2017. The dependent variable is a banking crisis indicator that takes a value equal to one for a banking crisis year as defined inBaron et al. (2021)and zero otherwise. For each country and yeart,Δ(Credit/GDP)is defined as the change in the ratio of private credit to GDP over the previous four years (t− 4 tot) andΔLoans arranged (retained) by foreign lendersas the log difference in the total amount of loans arranged (retained) by foreign lenders over the previous four years (t− 4 tot).Average market share arranged (retained)is defined as the average of the market share of active lenders who arranged (retained) loans in previous four years (t− 5 tot− 1). In columns (1) and (2) of panel B, we consider banking crises without lending booms, excluding any banking crises for which the change in the ratio of private credit to GDP over the precrisis period was below the median of the country–year observations. In columns (3) and (4) of panel B, we consider banking crises preceded by financial liberalizations. All variables are winsorized at 1% and 99% level. All regressions include country fixed effects. Standard errors are clustered by country. Robustt-statistics are shown in parentheses. ***, **, and * denote significance at the 1%, 5%, and 10% levels, respectively. In panel B, we repeat the tests considering crises with different characteristics. First, our sample includes a few crises that were not preceded by credit booms. In our baseline specifications, we consider these crises because less established lenders can contribute to the accumulation of bad loans and misallocation by substituting high market share lenders with sounder lending policies. Internet Table IA.VII shows that the results inTable 2are qualitatively invariant if we exclude crises that are not preceded by lending booms from the definition of precrisis periods. Columns (1) and (2) of panel B inTable 9show that not considering these crises also increases the relevance of the amount of credit extended by foreign and low market share banks for crisis predictions as theR2is somewhat larger than in the corresponding specifications in panel A. In columns (3) and (4), we consider crises that were preceded by financial liberalizations. Foreign and low market share lenders appear to have extended relatively more credit before these types of crises. Hence, the amount of credit extended by less established lenders should be more relevant to explain the occurrence of banking crises preceded by financial liberalizations than for general banking crises. Consistent with this conjecture, theR2in these specifications increases considerably in comparison with the corresponding specifications in panel A.11Overall, these findings support our conjecture that it would be important to consider the characteristics of the lenders to evaluate whether risk is accumulating in a country.
article.6. Mechanisms and Alternative Explanatio: 6.1. Expectations and Internalization of Externalities We have so far shown that foreign banks and banks with low market shares provide more credit and take relatively more risk than other lenders during precrisis periods. Based on existing theories, we have conjectured that lenders’ differential behavior may be driven either by different, and not necessarily rational, expectations on the borrowers’ future performance or by less established lenders’ lower propensity to internalize the negative spillovers created by the accumulation of risk in an economy. Whereas these explanations are not mutually exclusive and are likely to jointly contribute to the patterns we highlight, in what follows, we evaluate their relevance. In particular, we consider that lenders are likely to know some industries better than others and that this information is largely transferable across countries (Acharya et al. 2006,Loutskina and Strahan 2011,Blickle et al. 2021,Paravisini et al. 2023), especially for large borrowers in tradable industries. We, thus, expect that foreign and low market share banks largely fund tradable industries with which they are unfamiliar if their differential lending behavior during precrisis periods is driven by an information disadvantage and less accurate expectations about borrowers’ future performance. To test this conjecture, we construct a lender–country–industry–year panel and consider the total loan volume that industryiin countrycobtains from lenderbin yeart.We restrict the sample to tradable industries and estimate the equivalent ofEquation (1)in this more granular data set, including also a triple interaction term capturing different lenders’ propensity to extend credit to industries with which they have low familiarity during precrisis periods (and all lower order interaction terms). We capture industries with which a lender has low familiarity by defining aLow Familiaritydummy, which equals one if lenderbdid not extend any loans to industryiin any country in the previous three years and zero otherwise. Besides the sets of fixed effects included in the previous specifications, we additionally include interactions of industry and year fixed effects to control for the fact that some industries may perform particularly well during precrisis periods.12 We report the estimates inTable 10. In columns (1) and (2), the negative coefficient on the triple interaction termPrecrisis×Foreign Lender×Low Familiarityindicates that foreign lenders increase their exposure to borrowers in industries with which they are unfamiliar relatively less. Quite to the contrary, in columns (3) and (4), loan provision to low-familiarity industries increases with the lender’s market share.13Because negative externalities arising from distress and fire sales are particularly severe within a country’s industry (Carvalho 2015), high market share banks appear to enter industries in which risk accumulation and distress are likely to affect the rest of their portfolio to a lower extent. Hence, these findings suggest that the internalization of externalities is likely to play an important role in explaining why less established lenders extend more credit during precrisis periods. Table 10.Industry Familiarity Table 10.Industry Familiarity Notes.This table investigates whether lenders extend credit to industries with which they have low familiarity during precrisis periods. The estimates are from OLS regressions in a lender–country–industry–year panel. Industry is based at the two-digit SIC code level. We restrict the sample to tradable industries, in which knowledge is more transferable. The dependent variables,Loan Amount CommittedandAny Loan Committed, are computed considering the loans committed by lenderbto the borrowers in industryiin countrycin yeart.Low Familiaritytakes value equal to one if lenderbdid not extend any loans to industryiin the previous three years and zero otherwise. In columns (1) and (2), the variable lender is the Foreign dummy. In columns (3) and (4), the variable lender is the lender’sRetained Share. All regressions include interactions of lender and year fixed effects, of country and year fixed effects, and of industry and year fixed effects. Columns (3) and (4) control for the bank’s foreign status but the coefficient is not reported. The definitions of all variables are provided in Internet Appendix A. Standard errors are clustered by lender. Robustt-statistics are shown in parentheses. ***, **, and * denote significance at the 1%, 5%, and 10% levels, respectively. 6.2. Market Share vs. Alternative Lender Characteristics Finally, we consider to what extent a lender’s market share may be correlated with other lender characteristics that have been considered in previous literature. For instance, lenders that concentrate their portfolios in a few markets are believed to be more informed than diversified lenders (Acharya et al. 2006,Loutskina and Strahan 2011,Blickle et al. 2021). To capture this, we consider a lender’s portfolio share, defined as the dollar value of the loans that a lender arranges in a given country during a year, divided by the dollar value of all the loans arranged by that lender during that year. The correlation of a lender’s portfolio share with its arranged (retained) share is just 16% (14%), indicating that these proxies capture different aspects of banks’ business models. In Internet Table IA.IX, we run a horse race between market share and portfolio share, which helps to shed light on the mechanisms that lead high market share banks to extend fewer loans in precrisis periods. In column (2), specialized banks are less likely to enter in a syndicated loan during precrisis periods, but we find no statistically significant effect of lender specialization on the amount of credit committed in column (1). More importantly, the coefficient on the interaction between the precrisis dummy and market share indicates that high market share banks commit less credit during precrisis periods, supporting our earlier findings. Finally,Granja et al. (2022)highlight that distant small business loans are riskier for a bank and that greater lending distance is reflective of generalized risk taking. WhereasGranja et al. (2022)stress the effect of competition in a bank’s county of origin in the United States, we focus on economic conditions in the host country. Nevertheless, in Internet Table IA.X, we interact the geographical distance between the capital cities of the bank’s and the borrower’s countries with the precrisis dummy. Distant lending increases in precrisis periods, confirming the finding ofGranja et al. (2022). However, the effect of market share is unchanged.
article.7. Conclusions: Because rapid credit expansions may, or may not, precede banking crises, macroprudential policies face a trade-off between financial stability and financial deepening (Ayyagari et al. 2017,Richter et al. 2019). To identify the instances in which credit growth poses a threat to financial stability, it is important to investigate how credit booms that precede banking crises are different from other credit expansions. We show that the amount of credit extended by foreign lenders and low market share lenders increases to a larger extent than that by other lenders during credit booms that end up in crises. We find no evidence that this is the case during other credit expansions. Our results are important for the design of early warning systems and macroprudential policies. The buildup of leverage has been shown to be a key precursor of financial crises (e.g.,Gourinchas and Obstfeld 2012). Existing studies, however, focus on macrovariables. We highlight that a higher proportion of credit provided by less established banks can help identify the increases in debt that are more likely to impair financial stability, providing a rationale to increase capital requirements for all lenders that end up being directly or indirectly exposed. Alternatively, if countercyclical capital controls are used to prevent risk accumulation, our results suggest that taking into account who intermediates the capital inflows could be at least as important as the level of capital inflows. Capital inflows that increase lending by established banks could spur good lending booms. Taking this additional information into account could mitigate policymakers’ dilemma between financial stability and financial deepening.
